title: Gmail Smart Compose - System Design
description: A step-by-step ML system design walkthrough for real-time assisted email writing, grouped into the online suggestion path, offline ML pipeline, evaluation loop, and production rollout. It covers product triggering, privacy-safe data preparation, subword tokenization, decoder-only Transformers, two-stage training, low-latency beam-search serving, safety filtering, metrics, and model operations.
requirementsDiagram: User -->|tab accepts or keeps typing| Feedback

functional: Suggest the next few words while a user types an email body.
Start with a global English model and no per-user personalization.
Use the partial email body as the initial input, with room to add subject, recipient, and previous-message context later.
Show a suggestion only when the system is confident enough.
Filter suggestions that could expose sensitive information or make biased assumptions.
Collect acceptance and dismissal feedback for evaluation and future improvements.
nonFunctional: Return suggestions fast enough for typing, targeting roughly 100 ms end-to-end latency.
Scale to Gmail-like traffic: many users typing at once, with client-side triggering to avoid a request on every keystroke.
Train from roughly one billion email messages after privacy, language, and quality filtering.
Keep training data privacy-safe by removing PII, confidential content, duplicates, and non-English messages for the initial model.
Prefer predictable deterministic completions over creative variation.
Support safe rollout, rollback, and evaluation before exposing a new model broadly.

capacityDiagram: Emails[Up to 500 emails per heavy user per day]
QPS[Hundreds of thousands to millions of global peak QPS]

capacity.label: Registered users
capacity.note: Requirement-scale anchor; only a subset are composing at any moment.

capacity.label: Heavy-user upper bound
capacity.note: Useful for abuse and professional-user extremes, not the average user assumption.

capacity.label: Suggestion latency
capacity.note: The system must respond before the user naturally types the same words.

capacity.label: Fine-tuning corpus
capacity.note: After language detection, deduplication, redaction, and quality filtering.

capacity.label: Serving load
capacity.note: Client-side triggering, debouncing, and eligibility checks prevent a request per keystroke.

capacity.label: Request size
capacity.note: Context includes partial body and optional structured metadata, not full mailboxes.

capacity.label: Response size
capacity.note: Usually one short completion plus score, model version, and display metadata.

capacity.label: Model release cadence
capacity.note: Offline eval, safety review, canary, and A/B testing matter more than continuous retraining.

api.description: Return at most one inline completion for the current composing context.
diagram: C->>T: typing context after local trigger
S->>F: confidence length and safety checks

api.description: Record whether a shown suggestion was accepted, ignored, or explicitly dismissed.
Return client-side trigger thresholds, locale support, and rollout assignment.
diagram: Config->>R: lookup user cohort and locale

api.description: Register a trained model artifact after offline evaluation and safety review.
Move a model through canary, A/B test, general availability, or rollback states.

dataModel.name: sanitized_email_message
dataModel.note: Privacy-filtered email text used for fine-tuning, not raw user mail.

dataModel.name: tokenizer_version
dataModel.note: Subword vocabulary and normalization rules used by both training and serving.

dataModel.name: training_example
dataModel.note: Context and target tokens for next-token prediction.

dataModel.name: model_version
dataModel.note: A trained model artifact tied to a tokenizer and evaluation report.

dataModel.name: suggestion_log
dataModel.note: Privacy-minimized production log for latency, versioning, and quality metrics.

dataModel.name: feedback_event
dataModel.note: User interaction with a shown suggestion.

dataModel.name: evaluation_run
dataModel.note: Offline and online metrics for candidate and released models.

steps.title: 1. Online Path: Product Boundary and Triggering
steps.description: The online path starts by narrowing the product: Smart Compose is an inline assistant that suggests a short continuation while the user is already typing. The client should not call the backend on every keystroke; a lightweight triggering service decides when enough context exists to make a useful request.
steps.decisionPrompt: Where should the system decide whether to ask for a suggestion: in the Gmail client, in the backend, or both?

concepts.definition: A component that decides whether the current typing state is eligible for a suggestion.
concepts.whyItMatters: It cuts traffic and avoids distracting users with low-value completions.

concepts.definition: A response-time target tied to human typing speed rather than batch throughput.
concepts.whyItMatters: A good suggestion is useless if it arrives after the user has typed the same words.

options.name: Client-assisted triggering (default)
pros: Avoids backend calls when the prefix is too short or the user is typing continuously
Lets rollout config tune thresholds without shipping a new client
Keeps the backend focused on high-value suggestion requests
cons: Requires careful client implementation across web and mobile
Eligibility rules must remain simple enough to run locally

options.name: Backend-only triggering
pros: Centralizes trigger behavior
Simplifies older clients
cons: Creates far more network traffic
Backend receives many obviously unusable prefixes
Harder to meet global latency and cost targets

deepDives.title: Trigger examples
points: Do not trigger after one character or while the cursor is in the middle of a word.
Trigger after a pause, a whitespace boundary, or a phrase with enough context.
Suppress requests when locale is unsupported or the user disabled assisted writing.

bottlenecks.issue: A request per keystroke makes the design too expensive.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Use client-side debouncing, minimum context length, and server-side rate limits.

bottlenecks.issue: A slow backend creates UI flicker or stale suggestions.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Attach request sequence numbers and let the client discard responses for older prefixes.

talkingPoints: The API is allowed to return an empty suggestion; silence is part of the product.
The first design boundary is user experience and latency, not the model architecture.

flows.name: Eligible prefix request
flows.note: The client waits for enough context before crossing the network.
diagram: C->>T: user typed at word boundary

steps.title: 1a. Online Path: Real-Time Inference and Beam Search
steps.description: After triggering, the online path turns the current email context into a prompt, tokenizes it, runs the model, and generates a short completion. This is the inference-engine step: Smart Compose should use deterministic decoding, with beam search tracking several likely continuations and returning the highest-scoring short phrase.
steps.decisionPrompt: How should the serving path generate a completion while staying deterministic and under the latency budget?

concepts.definition: A deterministic decoding algorithm that keeps the top-k partial sequences at each generation step.
concepts.whyItMatters: It avoids the narrowness of greedy decoding while still producing predictable completions.

concepts.definition: The maximum number of recent tokens the model can use for one request.
concepts.whyItMatters: Serving must include enough recent email context without making inference too slow.

options.name: Beam search with short max length (default)
pros: Deterministic and predictable for the same input
Considers multiple likely phrases instead of only the top token
Works well because suggestions are short
cons: More expensive than greedy search
Beam width must be tuned to avoid latency spikes

options.name: Stochastic sampling
pros: More diverse output for creative writing products
Can explore lower-probability continuations
cons: Inconsistent results for the same prefix
Higher risk of surprising or inappropriate suggestions
Not a good fit for conservative email completion

deepDives.title: Prompt template
points: Start with body prefix only to match the initial requirement.
Add tagged optional fields later, such as subject, recipients, and previous thread snippets.
Train with missing optional fields so serving can use the same template flexibly.

bottlenecks.issue: Inference compute dominates cost at high QPS.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Use regional model replicas, accelerator-backed serving, small beam width, max token limits, and batching where latency permits.

bottlenecks.issue: A stale response can apply to a newer prefix.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Include prefix hash or request sequence in the response and validate it in the client.

talkingPoints: Greedy decoding is faster but can produce worse phrases because it commits to the top token too early.
Beam search is acceptable because Smart Compose wants short, conventional, deterministic completions.

flows.name: Beam-search suggestion
flows.note: The serving path generates candidates before product filters decide whether to show one.
diagram: participant B as Context Builder Service
C->>B: body prefix and optional context

steps.title: 1b. Online Path: Confidence, Safety, and Post-Processing
steps.description: The online path ends with a conservative product gate. Candidate completions are filtered by length, confidence, policy, and bias checks before the UI sees them, and returning no suggestion is a normal successful outcome.
steps.decisionPrompt: What checks must happen after generation but before showing text to the user?

concepts.definition: A minimum score below which the system suppresses a suggestion.
concepts.whyItMatters: Low-confidence text feels distracting and can damage trust.

concepts.definition: Rule-based or model-based filtering and rewriting after generation.
concepts.whyItMatters: The generated text must satisfy product safety and inclusivity requirements, not only language-model likelihood.

options.name: Conservative show-or-empty gate (default)
pros: Protects user trust by showing fewer weak suggestions
Keeps unsafe or biased output away from the UI
Easy to reason about in an interview
cons: May suppress useful suggestions
Threshold tuning requires online metrics and experiments

options.name: Always show best candidate
pros: Maximizes visible feature usage
Simple ranking logic
cons: Shows many low-quality completions
Raises safety and bias risk
Contradicts the requirement to show only confident suggestions

deepDives.title: Bias handling examples
points: Avoid assuming gender from a name unless explicit context supports it.
Prefer neutral wording when a completion would introduce sensitive attributes.
Suppress content that includes PII-like patterns or NSFW terms.

bottlenecks.issue: Over-filtering can make the feature feel absent.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Track show rate, acceptance rate, and suppression reasons by model version and locale.

bottlenecks.issue: Rule-only filters can miss nuanced unsafe output.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Use layered checks: rules for obvious cases, classifiers for nuance, and human evaluation for samples.

talkingPoints: Post-processing is not a replacement for safer training data; both are needed.
A blocked suggestion should not be treated as an error in the client.

flows.name: Suppress low-confidence suggestion
flows.note: Returning no suggestion is a normal successful outcome.
diagram: participant P as Safety Policy Service

steps.title: 2. Offline ML: Privacy-Safe Data Preparation
steps.description: The offline ML pipeline begins before model work: the training corpus must be made safe and useful. The pipeline combines general text for language understanding with email text for task adaptation, then filters for language, quality, deduplication, PII, confidential content, and policy-sensitive terms.
steps.decisionPrompt: What data is allowed to reach model training, and what must be removed before tokenization?

concepts.definition: Collect and retain only the data needed for the stated training purpose.
concepts.whyItMatters: Email content is sensitive, so raw mailbox data should not become a long-lived training artifact.

concepts.definition: A classifier or heuristic that tags the language of text before downstream processing.
concepts.whyItMatters: The initial product is English-only, so non-English data should not train or evaluate the first model.

options.name: Sanitized offline corpus (default)
pros: Creates a repeatable training set for offline experiments
Keeps raw email out of model training jobs
Supports privacy audits and redaction versioning
cons: Requires a strong data governance process
Training data may lag current email language trends

options.name: Train directly from production mail
pros: Simpler data movement in a prototype
Freshest possible data
cons: Unacceptable privacy and audit risk for email
Hard to reproduce experiments
Harder to remove confidential or duplicated content reliably

deepDives.title: Cleaning stages
points: Normalize whitespace, casing policy, signatures, quoted replies, and irrelevant symbols.
Remove duplicate boilerplate and repeated forwarded content so the model does not overfit.
Version redaction rules because evaluation needs to know exactly what preprocessing created the corpus.

bottlenecks.issue: PII redaction can miss edge cases.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Combine deterministic detectors, ML classifiers, sampling audits, and conservative exclusion rules.

bottlenecks.issue: Deduplication at billion-message scale is expensive.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Use content hashes, near-duplicate fingerprints, and partitioned batch processing.

talkingPoints: The training pipeline should make unsafe examples disappear before tokenization.
Privacy review is an architectural component here, not a legal afterthought.

flows.name: Email corpus sanitization
flows.note: Raw email is transformed into a restricted, reproducible training corpus.
diagram: participant E as Raw Email Store
participant R as PII Redaction Service
participant S as Sanitized Training Store

steps.title: 2a. Offline ML: Tokenization and Training Examples
steps.description: The offline ML pipeline next converts safe text into model-ready examples. A tokenizer converts text into token IDs, and training examples pair a context prefix with the next-token targets. Subword tokenization is the right default because it handles rare words better than word tokens and uses shorter sequences than character tokens.
steps.decisionPrompt: Which tokenization strategy should Smart Compose use, and how do we turn emails into next-token training examples?

concepts.definition: A method such as BPE, WordPiece, or SentencePiece that splits text into common word pieces.
concepts.whyItMatters: It can represent rare names and words without exploding the vocabulary.

concepts.definition: Training the model to predict each next token from the previous tokens.
concepts.whyItMatters: It matches the way Smart Compose generates completions one token at a time.

options.name: Subword tokenizer (default)
pros: Handles rare words and names without unknown-token failures
Keeps sequence length manageable
Works well with modern Transformer language models
cons: Requires versioned tokenizer artifacts
Pieces are less intuitive than full words during debugging

options.name: Word-level tokenizer
pros: Easy to explain and inspect
Short common phrases map cleanly to tokens
cons: Very large vocabulary at Gmail scale
Poor handling of misspellings, names, and rare terms
More unknown-token problems

deepDives.title: Context and target construction
points: Add a BOS token at the beginning and an EOS token at the end of each sequence.
For a sequence such as BOS Hi Petra EOS, the model learns BOS -> Hi, Hi -> Petra, and Petra -> EOS.
The same tokenizer version must be used in training and serving.

bottlenecks.issue: Changing tokenizer versions can invalidate model artifacts.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Store tokenizer_version on model_version and reject incompatible serving deployments.

bottlenecks.issue: Long emails can exceed the context window.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Use a recent-context window and optional prompt tags for subject or reply context.

talkingPoints: Tokenizer choice is a system contract because clients, training, and serving must agree on IDs.
Subword tokenization is a pragmatic middle ground between character and word tokenization.

flows.name: Build next-token examples
flows.note: One email sequence yields many supervised training positions.
diagram: participant S as Sanitized Training Store
participant E as Training Example Store
B->>E: context tokens and target tokens

steps.title: 2b. Offline ML: Decoder-Only Transformer Architecture
steps.description: With data represented as tokens, choose the model shape. Smart Compose is a text generation task, so a decoder-only Transformer predicts continuations from left to right: embeddings map token IDs into vectors, positional encodings preserve order, self-attention models context, and a prediction head returns probabilities over the vocabulary.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why is a decoder-only Transformer a better fit than an RNN, encoder-only model, or encoder-decoder model for short email completion?

concepts.definition: A mechanism that lets each token representation use information from earlier tokens in the sequence.
concepts.whyItMatters: It captures phrase-level context more effectively and parallelizes better than classic RNN recurrence.

concepts.definition: The final layer that converts Transformer outputs into probabilities for every token in the vocabulary.
concepts.whyItMatters: Smart Compose chooses continuations from these probabilities.

options.name: Decoder-only Transformer (default)
pros: Naturally generates left-to-right continuations
Parallelizes training across sequence positions
Handles longer context better than basic RNNs
cons: More expensive to serve than a small recurrent model
Needs careful latency optimization for interactive use

options.name: RNN or LSTM baseline
pros: Simpler and potentially smaller for very constrained devices
Can generate text autoregressively
cons: Harder to parallelize during training
Weaker at long-context dependencies
Not the modern default for large-scale language generation

deepDives.title: Fixed positional encoding
points: A Transformer does not inherently know token order, so order is added to embeddings.
Fixed sine-cosine encodings add no trainable parameters and can generalize to positions beyond the common training lengths.
Learned encodings may fit the training distribution well but can generalize poorly to unseen sequence lengths.

bottlenecks.issue: Large models can exceed the 100 ms budget.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Use a smaller distilled model, optimized serving runtime, batching, and accelerator-backed inference.

bottlenecks.issue: A full vocabulary softmax can be expensive.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Optimize model size and serving kernels; keep generated suggestions short.

talkingPoints: Encoder-only models such as BERT are better at understanding or classification than direct left-to-right generation.
Encoder-decoder models are useful for translation or summarization, but Smart Compose only needs continuation from a prefix.

flows.name: One next-token prediction
flows.note: The prediction head ranks candidate tokens from the current prefix.

steps.title: 2c. Offline ML: Two-Stage Training Pipeline
steps.description: The offline ML pipeline then trains and promotes candidates. Training from scratch on email alone is expensive and risks overfitting, so a two-stage pipeline pretrains a base language model on broad text, then fine-tunes it on sanitized email examples using the same next-token objective and cross-entropy loss.
steps.decisionPrompt: Should the team train only on email data, or pretrain generally and fine-tune on email?

concepts.definition: Reusing knowledge learned from a broad task and adapting it to a narrower task.
concepts.whyItMatters: The model learns general language once, then adapts to email tone and phrases.

concepts.definition: A loss function that penalizes the model when it assigns low probability to the correct next token.
concepts.whyItMatters: It directly trains the probability distribution used at serving time.

options.name: Pretrain then fine-tune (default)
pros: Improves generalization and reduces overfitting on email-only data
Expensive pretraining can be reused across related language tasks
Fine-tuning adapts the model to email style and phrases
cons: Requires managing multiple training stages and artifacts
General pretraining data must also pass quality and safety review

options.name: Train from scratch on email
pros: Simpler conceptual pipeline
Model specializes only in email data
cons: High compute cost and slower iteration
Greater risk of memorizing email-specific training examples
Weaker general language understanding

deepDives.title: Training objective
points: The model predicts every next token in the sequence, not only the final word.
Training computes losses for many token positions in parallel.
Fine-tuning keeps the same objective but changes the data distribution to email.

bottlenecks.issue: Training jobs can be costly and slow.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Reuse base models, checkpoint frequently, and run smaller candidate models before full training.

bottlenecks.issue: A candidate can look good offline while failing product safety.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Gate registry promotion on safety suites, bias tests, and human review samples.

talkingPoints: Perplexity and ExactMatch@N belong before production rollout, not after.
The tokenizer, model weights, data recipe, and metrics must be versioned together.

flows.name: Candidate model training
flows.note: The model only enters serving after objective metrics and safety checks pass.
diagram: participant G as General Text Store
participant E as Email Example Store

steps.title: 2d. Evaluation Loop: Feedback and Experimentation
steps.description: Evaluation connects offline model quality to production usefulness. Offline metrics such as perplexity and ExactMatch@N catch candidate quality before release; online metrics such as acceptance rate, usage rate, completion time, latency, and feedback rate measure real user impact.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do we prove a new Smart Compose model is better before and after production rollout?

concepts.definition: An offline metric based on how much probability the model assigns to the actual next tokens.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lower perplexity usually means the model predicts held-out text better.

concepts.definition: A controlled production comparison between model or product variants.
concepts.whyItMatters: Offline gains do not always translate to accepted suggestions or faster email composition.

options.name: Offline gates plus online A/B tests (default)
pros: Catches bad candidates before users see them
Measures real user behavior under production latency
Supports rollback based on guardrail metrics
cons: Slower than pushing every candidate directly
Requires experimentation infrastructure and metric discipline

options.name: Offline-only release
pros: Simpler pipeline
Faster initial launch for a prototype
cons: Cannot detect product regressions such as lower acceptance rate
Misses latency and interaction effects
Riskier for a high-traffic product

deepDives.title: Metric families
points: Offline: perplexity and ExactMatch@N on held-out sanitized email data.
Engagement: acceptance rate and usage rate.
Effectiveness: average completion time with and without suggestions.
Guardrails: p99 latency, feedback complaints, and safety suppression rates.

bottlenecks.issue: Feedback logs can accidentally capture sensitive text.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Log suggestion IDs, metadata, scores, and interaction events instead of raw draft content.

bottlenecks.issue: Acceptance rate alone can reward bland suggestions.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Combine acceptance with user time saved, human evaluation, safety metrics, and suppression reasons.

talkingPoints: ExactMatch@N is helpful because Smart Compose suggestions are short phrase completions.
Online evaluation should include latency because delayed suggestions are effectively wrong suggestions.

flows.name: Feedback to metrics
flows.note: Production interactions feed dashboards without storing raw draft text.

steps.title: 3. Operations: Model Rollout and Global Scale
steps.description: Operations make the design production-safe. A model version is released only when its tokenizer, model artifact, safety status, and rollout config are compatible. Serving fleets are regional, autoscaled, monitored, and able to fall back to returning no suggestion when overloaded.
steps.decisionPrompt: How should Gmail deploy model versions globally without breaking latency, safety, or tokenizer compatibility?

concepts.definition: Sending a small slice of traffic to a new version before broad release.
concepts.whyItMatters: A bad model or serving regression can be stopped before it affects most users.

concepts.definition: The system intentionally serves a simpler response when dependencies are slow or unhealthy.
concepts.whyItMatters: For Smart Compose, the safe degraded response is no suggestion.

options.name: Regional serving with canary rollout (default)
pros: Keeps inference close to users
Lets the team roll out and roll back by version, cohort, and locale
Contains failures to a small traffic slice first
cons: Model artifacts must be replicated and warmed in every serving region
Requires strong observability by model version and region

options.name: Single global serving region
pros: Simpler artifact deployment
Easier to debug one fleet
cons: High latency for many users
One regional incident can disable the feature globally
Harder to meet interactive p99 targets

deepDives.title: Rollout controls
points: Pin each request to a model_version and tokenizer_version.
Canary by percentage, locale, client platform, and geography.
Rollback should update routing config faster than replacing model binaries.

bottlenecks.issue: Cold model loads can spike latency.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Warm model replicas before shifting traffic and keep old versions loaded during canary.

bottlenecks.issue: Traffic bursts can overload accelerator capacity.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Autoscale serving pools, shed low-priority requests, and return empty suggestions on timeout.

talkingPoints: No suggestion is the right fallback because sending stale or unsafe text is worse than silence.
Tokenizer compatibility should be enforced automatically at model load time.

flows.name: Canary rollout and rollback
flows.note: The operator changes routing, not client behavior.
diagram: participant F as Regional Inference Fleet
participant A as Metrics and Alerts
R->>F: route traffic to previous model

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end Gmail Smart Compose architecture grouped by responsibility: the online suggestion path handles triggering, prompt construction, inference, decoding, filtering, display, and feedback; the offline ML pipeline sanitizes data, tokenizes examples, trains, evaluates, and registers models; the operations layer rolls out compatible model and tokenizer versions to regional serving fleets.

options.name: Global model with privacy-safe training and regional serving
pros: Matches the no-personalization starting requirement
Keeps serving latency low with regional inference fleets
Separates model quality gates from production rollout
Suppresses low-confidence or unsafe suggestions before display
cons: Inference remains expensive at Gmail scale
A global model cannot capture every user's personal writing style
Privacy and safety pipelines add release friction

options.name: Future personalized contextual model
pros: Can use recipient, subject, thread, and user-specific style to improve relevance
May increase acceptance rate for frequent senders and repetitive workflows
Supports multilingual and domain-specific extensions
cons: Raises privacy, consent, isolation, and fairness complexity
Needs stronger context retrieval and prompt construction
Harder to debug and evaluate because outputs vary by user context

deepDives.title: Why this is the final design
points: The online path is short: trigger, tokenize, infer, decode, filter, display.
The offline path is controlled: sanitize, tokenize, train, evaluate, register, roll out.
Every production response can safely be empty, which gives the system a clean fallback under low confidence, safety risk, timeout, or overload.

flows.name: End-to-end suggestion
flows.note: This is the normal user-facing path after model deployment.
diagram: participant I as Regional Inference Fleet

functional.requirement: Suggest the next few words while the user types.
functional.how: The client-assisted triggering path sends eligible prefixes to a regional suggestion service that generates short completions.

functional.requirement: Start with no personalization and English-only support.
functional.how: The initial corpus filters for English, and the online context starts with body-prefix prompts instead of user-specific style models.
Candidate completions pass length and confidence gates before they reach the UI.

functional.requirement: Avoid biased assumptions and unsafe suggestions.
functional.how: The design combines safe data preparation, policy filters, bias-sensitive post-processing, and human or automated safety evaluation.
The feedback service records accept, dismiss, latency, and model-version metadata without storing raw draft text.

nonFunctional.requirement: Meet the roughly 100 ms interactive latency target.
nonFunctional.how: Client-side triggering reduces load, regional inference keeps requests close, and beam width plus max token length are bounded.
The design avoids a backend call per keystroke, uses regional inference fleets, autoscaling, and an empty-suggestion fallback under overload.

nonFunctional.requirement: Train from around one billion email messages safely.
nonFunctional.how: Raw email goes through language filtering, redaction, deduplication, and sanitized storage before tokenization and fine-tuning.
The inference path chooses beam search rather than stochastic sampling.
The model registry, offline evaluation, canary rollout, version routing, and metrics alerts let operators promote or revert a model safely.

followUps: How would the design change if Smart Compose had to support multiple languages from day one?
How would you add personalization while preserving user privacy and avoiding data leakage?
What extra context would you add first: subject, recipient, previous thread, or calendar context?
How would you tune beam width, confidence threshold, and maximum suggestion length against latency?
How would you detect memorization of rare training phrases or sensitive text?
What should happen if the safety filter and model confidence disagree?
How would you make a smaller on-device model work for offline compose?
How would you evaluate whether Smart Compose saves time rather than merely increasing acceptance rate?
